content.text: PRR Exceptions and Risk Acceptance Documentation

No PRR process survives contact with reality unchanged. A security mitigation requires a third-party vendor that is two weeks away. The eval pass rate is 93% rather than 95% for one intent category. The on-call runbook exists but has not been dry-run. In each case, the choice is binary: delay the launch to fix the gap, or formally accept the risk with documented mitigations and a time-bound remediation commitment.

The distinction between a silent bypass and a formal exception is accountability. A silent bypass — marking a checklist item green when it is not — removes the risk from visibility and creates false confidence. A formal exception documents the gap, the reasoning, the mitigating controls, and the engineer who accepted the risk. If the remediation fails, the exception record is the audit trail.

attrs.alt: PRR exception decision flow and waiver lifecycle states

content.text: Severity Classification for Gaps

Before drafting a waiver, classify the gap by severity. Severity determines whether an exception is even possible, and if so, which approvers must sign off. A CRITICAL gap blocks launch unconditionally — no exception process applies. Exceptions are only available for MEDIUM and LOW severity gaps.

## PRR Gap Severity Classification

### CRITICAL — Launch blocked, no exception possible
Examples:
- Hardcoded production API key found in source code
- No output validation — LLM can return raw PII from retrieved documents
- No authentication on the inference endpoint (publicly accessible)
- Eval suite finds hallucinated API responses causing data corruption

Action: Fix before launch, no exception path.

### HIGH — Exception possible with VP Engineering approval only
Examples:
- Circuit breaker not configured (fallback exists but no auto-open threshold)
- Eval pass rate below 85% (not 95%) in a high-traffic intent category
- Security threat model not completed (assessment in progress)
- No on-call rotation (one engineer is the single point of failure)

Approver: Engineering Lead + VP Engineering
Waiver window: max 7 days from launch

### MEDIUM — Exception possible with Engineering Lead + domain approver
Examples:
- Eval pass rate 93–94% (below 95%) in a low-traffic intent category
- Daily token budget alert not yet wired to PagerDuty (manual monitoring in place)
- Runbook exists but not dry-run (scheduled within 14 days)
- Shadow eval configured but not yet deployed

Approver: Engineering Lead + domain approver (SRE / ML Eng / Security / FinOps)
Waiver window: max 30 days from launch

### LOW — Exception with Engineering Lead approval only
Examples:
- Dashboard exists but one non-critical panel is missing
- Cost projection uses P50 estimate only (P99 to be added within 7 days)
- Synthetic test data has not been reviewed by legal yet (in queue)

Approver: Engineering Lead
Waiver window: max 14 days from launch
Waiver Document Template

Each exception is recorded as a structured document attached to the launch ticket. The template enforces completeness — a waiver missing any required field is invalid and cannot be approved. The PRR coordinator is responsible for filling in the template and routing it to the correct approvers.

## PRR Waiver

**Waiver ID:** WAI-2026-0042
**Service:** genai-chat-service
**Version:** v1.0
**PRR Domain:** Quality / Evaluation
**Severity:** MEDIUM
**Created by:** @ml-dave (ML Engineer)
**Created date:** 2026-07-28

---

### 1. Gap Description
The eval suite passes at 93.2% against the production model (gpt-4o-2024-08-06,
prompt sha a3f9c11), below the 95.0% pass threshold.

The failing cases are concentrated in the "account cancellation" intent category
(18 of 21 failures). This category represents 0.3% of production traffic based
on the past 30 days of staging traffic.

### 2. Root Cause
The "account cancellation" training cases in the eval set include ambiguous
phrasings ("I want to stop" / "please cancel my thing") that the current
prompt does not handle well. A targeted prompt improvement is in progress.

### 3. Risk Assessment
**Impact if this gap manifests in production:**
Users with account-cancellation intent receive an unhelpful or misdirected response.
No data corruption, PII leakage, or financial harm identified.

**Likelihood of manifestation:**
High — the failing cases are reproducible in staging with real user phrasings.

**User impact at current traffic levels:**
Approximately 3 users per 1,000 may receive a degraded response for
account-cancellation queries.

### 4. Mitigating Controls
1. Human-agent fallback is active for all "account cancellation" intents at launch.
   Any response with confidence < 0.75 on this intent is escalated to a human agent.
2. Shadow eval is scheduled to run against production traffic from day 1.
   Alert fires if quality score drops below 0.88 for this intent category.
3. The "account cancellation" intent category is excluded from SLO error budget
   calculations until the remediation is shipped (tracked in SLO config separately).

### 5. Remediation Plan
- **Fix:** Expand eval set with 30 additional account-cancellation cases and
  update the system prompt with explicit cancellation handling instructions.
- **Owner:** @ml-dave
- **Deadline:** 2026-08-11 (14 calendar days from launch)
- **Verification:** Re-run full eval suite; pass rate must reach ≥ 95%.
  If not reached by deadline, the account-cancellation flow is gated behind
  a feature flag until the threshold is met.

### 6. Approvals Required

| Role              | Approver      | Status       | Date       |
|-------------------|---------------|--------------|------------|
| ML Engineer       | @ml-dave      | ✅ Confirmed | 2026-07-28 |
| Engineering Lead  | @lead-grace   | ⏳ Pending   | —          |

**Waiver expires:** 2026-08-11

---
_This waiver was created using the PRR exception process. Any question about
this risk decision should be directed to @lead-grace (Engineering Lead)._
Tracking Waivers to Closure

An approved waiver is a liability on the service's risk register. Waivers must be tracked to one of two terminal states: REMEDIATED (the fix was shipped and the PRR check now passes) or EXPIRED/VIOLATED (the remediation date passed without a fix). The waiver tracking script runs weekly and alerts the service owner for any open waivers approaching expiry.

// prr-waivers.json — version-controlled waiver register for the service.
// Each entry is a waiver. Status transitions: DRAFT → APPROVED → REMEDIATED (or EXPIRED).
[
  {
    "id": "WAI-2026-0042",
    "service": "genai-chat-service",
    "domain": "quality_eval",
    "severity": "MEDIUM",
    "summary": "Eval pass rate 93.2% (below 95%) for account-cancellation intent",
    "status": "APPROVED",
    "created": "2026-07-28T09:00:00Z",
    "expires": "2026-08-11T23:59:59Z",
    "approvers": ["@ml-dave", "@lead-grace"],
    "remediation_owner": "@ml-dave",
    "remediation_ticket": "ENG-5532",
    "mitigations": [
      "human-agent fallback active for account-cancellation",
      "shadow eval alert configured at 0.88 threshold"
    ]
  }
]

Commit prr-waivers.json to the service repository alongside prr-status.json. This makes the waiver register visible to the whole team in code review, searchable in git history, and auditable by security teams without needing access to a ticket tracker.
What Cannot Be Waived

Some PRR gaps are not eligible for the exception process regardless of business pressure. These absolute blockers exist because the risk of proceeding is not bounded — a hardcoded API key can leak indefinitely, a missing output validator can exfiltrate PII at scale, and a missing authentication layer means the endpoint is publicly exploitable.
Hardcoded production API keys or credentials anywhere in source code, build artifacts, or container images — CRITICAL, no waiver.
Missing output validation where the LLM response is passed directly to users without any filtering — CRITICAL, no waiver.
Unauthenticated inference endpoint accessible from the public internet — CRITICAL, no waiver.
No fallback response path for LLM outages (service returns 500 with no user-facing message) — CRITICAL, no waiver.
Eval suite that has never run against any version of the production model — no baseline, no waiver (run the suite first, even once).

When a stakeholder asks you to waive a CRITICAL gap, the correct response is: 'This gap is not eligible for a waiver — it needs to be fixed or the launch must be delayed.' Accepting CRITICAL gaps under business pressure is how production incidents happen.
CI Enforcement of Waiver Expiry

Waivers with no automated enforcement become forgotten promises. A CI job that reads prr-waivers.json on every main-branch merge and fails if any waiver is violated or if a feature protected by a waiver is active without the waiver being current creates a forcing function: either ship the remediation or the pipeline blocks you.

# .github/workflows/waiver-enforcement.yml
# Runs on every push to main and every nightly schedule.
# Fails if any waiver is violated (expired without remediation).
name: PRR Waiver Enforcement

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - "prr-waivers.json"
      - "config/model-config.prod.yaml"
      - "app/**"
  schedule:
    - cron: "0 6 * * *"    # 06:00 UTC daily — morning alert for overnight violations

jobs:
  check-waivers:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install requests python-dateutil

      - name: Check waiver status
        run: python3 scripts/waiver_tracker.py
        env:
          SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL: ${{ secrets.PRR_SLACK_WEBHOOK }}

      - name: Verify waiver-gated features are flagged off if waiver violated
        run: python3 scripts/check_waiver_feature_flags.py
        env:
          LAUNCHDARKLY_SDK_KEY: ${{ secrets.LD_SDK_KEY }}

# scripts/check_waiver_feature_flags.py
# Verifies that any feature protected by an EXPIRED/VIOLATED waiver
# is gated off in LaunchDarkly. Fails CI if a violated waiver's feature
# is still live.

import json, sys, os, pathlib
import datetime
import ldclient
from ldclient.config import Config

WAIVERS_FILE = pathlib.Path("prr-waivers.json")

sdk_key = os.environ["LAUNCHDARKLY_SDK_KEY"]
ldclient.set_config(Config(sdk_key))
client = ldclient.get()

if not client.is_initialized():
    print("ERROR: LaunchDarkly client failed to initialize")
    sys.exit(1)

waivers = json.loads(WAIVERS_FILE.read_text()) if WAIVERS_FILE.exists() else []
now = datetime.datetime.now(tz=datetime.timezone.utc)
errors = []

for waiver in waivers:
    if waiver["status"] not in ("APPROVED",):
        continue   # only check currently-approved waivers

    expiry = datetime.datetime.fromisoformat(waiver["expires"].replace("Z", "+00:00"))
    if expiry >= now:
        continue   # not expired yet — ok

    # Waiver has expired — check if the protected feature flag is still on
    feature_flag = waiver.get("feature_flag")
    if not feature_flag:
        errors.append(f"[{waiver['id']}] Waiver expired on {waiver['expires'][:10]} with no remediation and no feature_flag to gate.")
        continue

    flag_value = client.variation(feature_flag, {"key": "ci-check"}, default=False)
    if flag_value:
        errors.append(
            f"[{waiver['id']}] Waiver for '{waiver['domain']}' expired on {waiver['expires'][:10]}, "
            f"but feature flag '{feature_flag}' is still ON. Gate the feature or ship the remediation."
        )

client.close()

if errors:
    print("WAIVER ENFORCEMENT FAILURES:")
    for e in errors:
        print(f"  - {e}")
    sys.exit(1)

print("Waiver feature-flag enforcement: PASS")
Retrospective Integration

Every waiver that is closed — whether REMEDIATED on time or EXPIRED/VIOLATED — should be reviewed in the next team retrospective. The goal is not blame, but process improvement: why did the gap exist at launch, was the waiver process followed correctly, and what would prevent the same gap class in future PRRs?

## PRR Waiver Retrospective Template

**Waiver ID:** WAI-2026-0042
**Review date:** 2026-09-01 (sprint retro following remediation)

### What was the gap?
Eval pass rate 93.2% vs 95% threshold for account-cancellation intent.

### Was it caught early enough?
No — the eval suite was run 3 days before the launch deadline, leaving
insufficient time to improve the prompt and re-run. The eval gate should be run
2 weeks before launch, not 3 days.

### Did the mitigating controls work?
Yes — the human-agent fallback handled all affected queries. Shadow eval confirmed
the failure rate matched our estimate (0.3% of traffic).

### Was the remediation shipped on time?
Yes — shipped on day 11 of the 14-day window. Eval now passes at 96.1%.

### What would prevent this next time?
- Add a "soft warn" at 90% in the CI eval gate (currently only fails at 85%)
  so teams get earlier signal before the hard 95% PRR threshold is reached.
- Schedule the PRR eval gate run at T-14 days, not T-3 days.

### Action items
- [ ] Update CI eval gate: add warning at 90% — @ml-dave — due 2026-09-15
- [ ] Add eval gate to the launch timeline template — @lead-grace — due 2026-09-08

Track action items from waiver retrospectives in the same ticket tracker as the service PRR. Action items that are not tracked to completion repeat the same gap in the next launch cycle — the retrospective is only useful if it drives systematic improvements.
Summary
PRR exceptions are formal, not informal — a gap is either fixed before launch or covered by an approved waiver with a named owner, mitigating controls, and a hard expiry date.

Severity drives the exception path: CRITICAL gaps block launch unconditionally; HIGH gaps require VP Engineering approval with a 7-day waiver window; MEDIUM and LOW gaps require Engineering Lead plus domain approver with 30- and 14-day windows respectively.
The waiver template has six required sections: gap description, root cause, risk assessment, mitigating controls, remediation plan, and approvals — a waiver missing any section is not valid.
Track waivers in prr-waivers.json alongside the codebase; run waiver_tracker.py weekly to alert the service owner when waivers approach or pass their expiry date.
A violated waiver (expiry passed, remediation not shipped) requires immediate action: gate the affected feature behind a feature flag or roll back — the risk is no longer accepted.

Four gap types can never be waived: hardcoded credentials, missing output validation, unauthenticated inference endpoints, and missing LLM fallback paths — these are absolute blockers regardless of business pressure.
